text:
While trusting and being influenced by scientists is a prerequisite for both individual and collective evidence-based decision making — and generally a cornerstone of meeting the challenges that our world currently faces (climate, pandemics, etc.)— it is often narrated in the media as being in a crisis. In an effort to test that claim, we collected in a pre-registered massive survey exercise spanning 68 countries with a sample size of 71 922 participants.

We disagree with most of the existing accounts of the distrust of science. In nearly all of the countries in our data set, the public trusts scientists a moderate amount. There is widespread endorsement of the view that scientists should participate more in public debates and in the political process, and an almost universal acceptance of the view that using the scientific method to find the truth is the best way to do so. In this paper we discover several individual level predictors of trust in scientists. In particular, trusting science more are: women, rich people, liberals, people who reject science-related populist ideas, and those with a low social dominance motivation. In most of the Christian countries in our sample, trust is negatively related to religiosity but this relationship is often positive in the Muslim countries in our sample. Of all the variables that we use, the level of formal education has the smallest and least consistent impact on the trust that a person has for scientists.

Analysis of the data shows also a clear discrepancy between the priorities of the public and those of the scientists themselves. The public would have scientists to concentrate on improving health, on finding new energy sources and on combating poverty, while believing at the same time that too many scientists are engaged on defence and military work. The overall level of public trust in scientists is still high but our analysis shows that even a minority who distrust scientists can have enough influence to prevent evidence-based policy from being implemented. These findings are crucial in that they provide a new benchmark from which scientists, and science communicators, can work to try and improve the relationship between the public and science.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
